verdict_reasoning: KLK2 (kallikrein-related peptidase 2, also known as hK2) is a secreted serine protease expressed predominantly in prostate epithelium. It is synthesized as a prepro-enzyme, processed through the secretory pathway, and secreted. However, KLK2 is well-documented to associate wash-resistantly with the cell surface of prostate cancer cells via binding to protease inhibitors (notably PAI-1) complexed with surface receptors (LRP1/megalin and other endocytic receptors), and via interaction with the extracellular matrix components anchored to prostate epithelial surfaces. More critically for triage: multiple antibody-based and bispecific therapeutic programs (e.g., PSMA/hK2 bispecifics, anti-hK2 ADCs) have been developed that target hK2 on the cell surface of prostate cancer cells, with flow cytometry and surface staining on intact LAPC4 and other prostate cancer cell lines confirming surface accessibility. The classical secreted + surface-retained biology most closely fits 'dual_localization' (dominant secreted pool, but documented PM-associated pool). However, the TM-precursor/surface-retention evidence is strong enough for contextual classification.
predicted_key_uncertainty: KLK2 has no transmembrane domain; surface association is via non-covalent interaction with surface receptors/matrix. The wash-resistance and stability of this association is debated, making yes vs contextual a judgment call.
